chapter_summary: This is the asana (posture) catalogue of the Gheranda Samhita (chapter 2, verses GhS_2.7-GhS_2.27), the seated and reclining seats of Gheranda's seven-limbed (saptanga) Hatha-Yoga. Each posture is defined by exact anatomical construction and assigned a specific fruit. Siddhasana: heel placed at the perineum, chin set on the heart, body made motionless (sthanu), senses restrained, gaze fixed between the eyebrows with an unmoving eye; it is declared the seat that breaks open the door-leaf of liberation (mokshakapata-bhedana). Padmasana: each foot crossed onto the opposite thigh, hands gripping the big toes from behind, thumbs at the heart, chin down, gaze on the nose-tip; destroyer of disease and disorder. Bhadrasana: ankles below the scrotum reversed, big toes held by the hands from behind, jalandhara (throat-lock) applied, gaze on the nose-tip; destroyer of all disease. Muktasana: left ankle at the anus, right ankle above, head-neck-body aligned; bestower of siddhi. Vajrasana: thighs made firm like a thunderbolt, both feet beside the anus; granter of siddhi to yogis. Svastikasana: soles tucked between thigh and knee, body erect. Simhasana (lion): ankles below the scrotum reversed and turned upward, palms on the ground, knees apart, mouth open, throat-lock applied, gaze on the nose-tip; destroyer of all disease. Gomukhasana: feet placed by the buttocks on either side, body steady, shaped like a cow's face. Virasana: one foot set on the opposite thigh, the other tucked behind. Dhanurasana: legs stretched straight like a staff, hands holding the feet behind, body bent like a bow. Shavasana (corpse, also mritasana): lying supine on the ground like a corpse; remover of fatigue and bringer of rest to the mind. Guptasana: feet hidden between the knees and thighs, seat covering the anus. Matsyasana (fish): from freed lotus, lying back with the head wrapped by the elbows; destroyer of disease. Matsyendrasana: belly drawn in (paschima tana), left foot on the right knee, right elbow there, gaze between the eyebrows. Gorakshasana: feet upturned between knees and thighs, ankles covered by the upturned hands, throat-contraction applied, gaze on the nose-tip; cause of siddhi for yogis. Yogendra-pitha / Paschimottanasana: legs stretched out like a staff, forehead laid between the legs, feet gripped by the hands. Utkatasana: balancing on the toes with heels raised, the seat resting above. Throughout, a precise bodily lock (chin, throat, or genital seat) is repeatedly coupled with a single fixed inward gaze (nose-tip or brow-center) and a named result — health, freedom from disorder, or attainment (siddhi/moksha).

story.title: The Sage's Catalogue of Seats: From the Corpse to the Door of Liberation
story.arc: Sage Gheranda lays out a graded gallery of bodily seats, each a precise lock of limb, throat and gaze. The arc moves from the most charged — Siddhasana, whose heel at the root and gaze between the brows 'breaks the door-leaf of liberation' — through a long body of postures that promise the lesser but indispensable gifts of health and freedom from disorder (Padmasana, Bhadrasana, Simhasana, Matsyasana), past the great equalizer Shavasana where the yogi lies as a corpse to still the mind, and on to the seats that grant siddhi (Vajrasana, Muktasana, Gorakshasana). The hidden through-line: every posture is built so the body becomes a stable, sealed vessel and the attention is bent inward to a single point — nose-tip or brow-center.
story.timeline_note: Instructional, not narrative — a sequential enumeration of postures GhS_2.7 through GhS_2.27 within the asana limb of the saptanga yoga.
story.comic_potential: Low — a technical posture manual; any lightness lives only in vivid images like the corpse pose (shavasana) and the lion's open-mouthed roar-seat (simhasana).

teachings.teaching: Siddhasana is singled out above all: heel pressed at the perineum, chin locked to the heart, body made motionless (sthanu), gaze steady between the eyebrows — and this alone is named the posture that breaks open the door-leaf of liberation (mokshakapata-bhedana).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga lens this is the literal architecture of the practice, not metaphor. The heel sealing the root closes the lower gate so prana cannot leak downward; the chin-lock (jalandhara) closes the upper gate; with both gates sealed the energy has only one road — upward through sushumna. The gaze fixed between the brows is attention parked at the doorway. 'Breaking the door-leaf of moksha' is exact: the posture mechanically converts the body into a closed circuit so the awakened force can split the bolt of the inner door. Sharma teaches that liberation is engineered by sealing the body and turning the gaze around, not merely believed into being.

teachings.teaching: Almost every posture pairs a physical lock with a single fixed gaze point — nose-tip (nasagra) in Padmasana, Bhadrasana, Simhasana, Gorakshasana, or brow-center (bhru-madhya) in Siddhasana and Matsyendrasana.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the drishti is the operative instruction; the limbs only hold the body still so the gaze can do its work. A wandering eye drags the mind; a fixed eye fixes the mind. Pinning attention to nose-tip or brow-center is the first deliberate withdrawal of awareness from the outgoing senses inward toward the spinal channel. The asana is scaffolding; the steady gaze is where the actual yoga begins.

teachings.teaching: Most postures are credited with curing disease and disorder — Padmasana destroys vyadhi-vikara, Bhadrasana and Simhasana destroy all disease, Matsyasana removes disease, Shavasana removes fatigue and rests the mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads health here not as the goal but as the precondition. A diseased, restless body cannot sustain the long stillness the higher kriya demands, so the system first hands the seeker postures that make the instrument sound. The 'destruction of disease' is the clearing of the first obstacle (the body itself), and Shavasana — lying like a corpse to still the chitta — is the bridge: only a body that can rest completely can be entered consciously.

teachings.teaching: Certain seats are named specifically as granters of siddhi for the yogi — Muktasana is siddhi-giving, Vajrasana grants the yogi siddhi, Gorakshasana is declared a cause of attainment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework siddhi is not stage magic but the matured result of a sealed body holding steady prana long enough for the inner process to ripen. The thunderbolt seat (Vajrasana) and the seat named for Gorakshanath of the Nath line are flagged because their geometry — feet at the root, throat contracted, gaze inward — most efficiently fuses the downward and upward currents. Sharma's caution applies: these fruits ripen only inside the living Guru-disciple bond; the verse names the seat, not the secret turning of force the seat is built to hold.
